passages.explanation: Six days later, Jesus takes Peter, James, and John up a high mountain by themselves. He's transfigured before them — his face shines like the sun, his clothes become white as light. Moses and Elijah appear, talking with him. Peter says: Lord, it's good for us to be here. If you want, I'll make three tents — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah. While he's still speaking, a bright cloud overshadows them. A voice from the cloud says: this is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased. Listen to him. The disciples fall on their faces, terrified. Jesus comes and touches them: get up, don't be afraid. When they look up, they see no one except Jesus alone. Moses represents the Law, Elijah the Prophets — and both defer to Jesus. The Father's command is simple: listen to him. Not to them anymore. Him.

As they come down the mountain, Jesus commands them: tell no one the vision until the Son of Man has risen from the dead. The disciples ask: why do the scribes say Elijah must come first? Jesus answers: Elijah is indeed coming and will restore all things. But I tell you Elijah has already come, and they didn't recognise him — they did to him whatever they wanted. Even so the Son of Man will also suffer at their hands. Then the disciples understand he's speaking about John the Baptiser. The pattern is set: the forerunner was rejected and killed. The one he pointed to will follow the same path.

When they come to the multitude, a man kneels before Jesus: Lord, have mercy on my son. He's epileptic and suffers grievously — he often falls into the fire and the water. I brought him to your disciples, and they couldn't cure him. Jesus answers: faithless and perverse generation, how long will I be with you? Bring him to me. Jesus rebukes the demon and it goes out. The boy is cured from that hour. The disciples come privately: why couldn't we cast it out? Jesus says: because of your little faith. If you have faith as a grain of mustard seed, you'll tell this mountain to move and it will. Nothing will be impossible for you. But this kind doesn't go out except by prayer and fasting. The disciples had been given authority in chapter 10 — but authority without faith and prayer becomes empty mechanics.

While they're gathering in Galilee, Jesus tells them: the Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men. They will kill him, and on the third day he will be raised. The disciples are exceedingly sorry. This is the second passion prediction. The disciples hear the death part but don't engage with the resurrection part. Grief drowns out hope.

When they come to Capernaum, the collectors of the two-drachma temple tax come to Peter: doesn't your teacher pay the temple tax? Peter says yes. When he comes into the house, Jesus speaks first: what do you think, Simon? From whom do the kings of the earth collect tolls or taxes — from their sons or from strangers? Peter says: from strangers. Jesus says: then the sons are exempt. But so we don't cause them to stumble, go to the sea, cast a hook, take the first fish that comes up, open its mouth, and you'll find a coin. Take that and give it for me and you. Jesus asserts his freedom — as the Son, he doesn't owe temple tax to his Father's house — but voluntarily pays to avoid unnecessary offence. Principle and wisdom working together.
